q: What did students learn in Grade 11 regarding an object in circular motion?
a: Angular displacement, angular velocity, and angular acceleration.
Linear displacement, linear velocity, and linear acceleration.
Tangential velocity, tangential acceleration, and centripetal acceleration.
Rotational kinetic energy, torque, and angular momentum.

q: What concepts will be studied in this chapter for rotational motion?
a: Constant angular acceleration and centripetal acceleration.
Moment of inertia and rotational kinetic energy.

q: What is a key learning outcome for students in this chapter regarding types of motion?
a: Differentiate between circular motion and rotational motion.
Differentiate between translational motion and vibrational motion.
Differentiate between projectile motion and parabolic motion.
Differentiate between simple harmonic motion and wave motion.

q: Which aspect of rotational motion is expected for students to examine in this chapter?
a: Rotational motion under constant angular acceleration.
Rotational motion with variable angular acceleration.

q: What is another learning outcome related to quantities in this chapter?
a: Deduce the relations between angular and linear quantities.
Deduce the relations between energy and momentum.
Deduce the relations between force and work.
Deduce the relations between power and efficiency.

q: What specific type of acceleration is a learning outcome for students to understand?
What distinction are students expected to make regarding circular motion?
a: Distinguish between uniform circular motion and nonuniform circular motion.
Distinguish between conservative and non-conservative forces in circular motion.
Distinguish between open and closed circular paths.
Distinguish between internal and external forces in circular motion.

q: In circular motion, how does an object move?
a: The object just moves in a circle.
The object rotates about an axis within itself.
The object oscillates back and forth along a circular arc.
The object moves in a helical path.

q: What is an example of an object that moves in a circle at a constant height, as mentioned in the text?
a: Artificial satellites going around the earth.
The rotation of the Earth about its own axis.

q: In rotational motion, how does the object behave?
a: The object rotates about an axis.
The object moves in a straight line.
The object maintains a fixed orientation in space.

q: Which phenomenon is an example of rotational motion that creates the cycle of day and night?
a: Rotation of earth about its own axis.
Motion of the blades of a fan.

q: What defines a rigid body?
a: A body that does not deform or change shape.
A body that always moves in a straight line.
A body whose mass changes during motion.
A body that can easily be compressed.

q: What characteristic of a rigid body remains constant, regardless of how the body moves?
a: The distance between any two particles within the body.

q: When a rigid body is in rotational motion, what happens to all the particles constituting it?
a: They undergo circular motion about a common axis.
They move in parallel straight lines.
They move randomly without a common pattern.

q: What occurs when an object (rigid body) rotates and speeds up or slows down?
a: Its angular velocity is changing, leading to angular acceleration.

q: What condition must be met for angular acceleration to be considered constant?
a: If its angular velocity changes at a constant rate.
If its linear velocity changes at a constant rate.
If its position changes linearly with time.

q: What is the motion called when an object's angular acceleration is constant?
a: Rotational motion under constant angular acceleration.

q: Which variables are considered rotational variables that were previously introduced?
a: Angular displacement, angular velocity, and angular acceleration.
Linear displacement, linear velocity, and linear acceleration.

q: In this section, for what type of motion are rotational variables applied to analyze?
a: Rotational motion for a rigid body about a fixed axis under a constant angular acceleration.
Rotational motion of a deformable body.
Translational motion of a point mass.

q: What type of equations can be derived for an object that rotates with a constant angular acceleration?
a: Equations of motion for projectile trajectory.

q: Kinematic equations for rotational motion are expressed in terms of which quantities at any instant of time?
a: Angular displacement, angular velocity, and angular acceleration.
Linear displacement, linear velocity, and linear acceleration.
Torque, moment of inertia, and angular momentum.

q: If $\omega_0$ is the initial angular speed at $t = 0$, how is angular speed $\omega$ at time $t$ derived for constant angular acceleration?
What is the formula for average angular speed?
The angular displacement for a time interval $t$ is initially expressed as $\theta = \bar{\omega} t$. Which two equations are used to further express $\theta$ and $\omega$?
Which kinematic equation relates angular displacement, initial angular speed, angular acceleration, and time?
Which kinematic equation relates final angular speed, initial angular speed, angular acceleration, and angular displacement?
What is the relationship between the equations of motion for constant angular acceleration and those for linear motion?
a: They are the same, with angular quantities replaced by corresponding linear ones.
They are inverse of each other.
They are only applicable under specific, different conditions.

q: What is the linear counterpart of angular displacement ($\theta$)?
What is the linear counterpart of angular velocity ($\omega$)?
What is the linear counterpart of angular acceleration ($\alpha$)?
According to Table 1.1, what is the angular equivalent of the linear equation $v = v_0 + at$?
According to Table 1.1, what is the angular equivalent of the linear equation $s = v_0 t + \frac{1}{2} a t^2$?
According to Table 1.1, what is the angular equivalent of the linear equation $v^2 = v_0^2 + 2as$?
According to Table 1.1, what is the angular equivalent of the linear equation $\bar{v} = \frac{v + v_0}{2}$?
Under what condition is the angular acceleration of a rotating object equal to zero?
a: When there is no change in angular velocity.
When the angular velocity is increasing.
When the angular velocity is decreasing.
When the object is at rest.

q: The term "rotational motion" refers to an object's rotation about what?
a: A fixed point in space, without rotation.

q: "Angular displacement" is a key word referring to what?
a: The angle through which an object rotates.
The linear distance an object moves.
The rate of change of angular position.
The rate of change of angular velocity.

q: "Angular velocity" is defined as what?
a: The rate of change of angular displacement.
The rate of change of linear displacement.
The rate of change of angular acceleration.

q: "Angular acceleration" is defined as what?
a: The rate of change of angular velocity.
The rate of change of linear velocity.
The linear distance covered over time.

q: Which of the following describes circular motion?
a: An object just moves in a circle.
An object rotates about an axis.
An object moves in a straight line.
An object vibrates back and forth.

q: What types of quantities are related in Section 1.2?
a: Linear quantities (s, v, a) and angular quantities ($\theta$, $\omega$, $\alpha$).

q: In rotational motion, how do all particles of an object rotating around the axis of rotation move?
If $s$ is the linear distance of a particle of a rotating object measured along the arc, and $\theta$ is the angle subtended at the origin, what is the relation between $s$ and $\theta$?
In the relation $s = r \theta$, what must $\theta$ be measured in?
For a rotational object, how does the instantaneous angular velocity $\omega$ compare for all the particles comprising the object?
a: It is the same for all particles.
It is zero for particles at the edge.
It is infinite for particles near the axis.

q: What is the relation between the magnitude of the linear velocity $v$ and the angular velocity $\omega$ of a particle?
In the relation $v = r \omega$, what must $\omega$ be measured in?
To which particles of a rotational object does the relation $v = r \omega$ apply?
a: To every particle of the rotational object.
Only to particles at the axis of rotation.
Only to particles at the outermost edge.
Only to particles moving in a straight line.

q: What is the direction of linear velocity relative to the path of a rotating object?
a: Perpendicular to the path, pointing inwards.
Perpendicular to the path, pointing outwards.

q: What is another name for linear velocity in the context of rotational motion?
What happens if the magnitude of the angular velocity of a rotating object changes?
a: The magnitude of linear velocity will also change, and the object will have tangential acceleration.
The object will have only centripetal acceleration.

q: What is the direction of tangential acceleration?
a: Always tangent to the circular path.
Always perpendicular to the circular path, pointing inwards.
Always perpendicular to the circular path, pointing outwards.
Always along the axis of rotation.

q: How does tangential acceleration $a_T$ relate to angular acceleration $\alpha$?
In the equation $a_T = r \alpha$, what must angular acceleration $\alpha$ be measured in?
What does the tangential acceleration of a rotating object measure?
a: How fast the tangential velocity changes.
How fast the angular displacement changes.
How fast the angular velocity changes.

q: According to Table 1.2, what is the relation for displacement ($s$)?
According to Table 1.2, what is the relation for tangential velocity ($v$)?
According to Table 1.2, what is the relation for tangential acceleration ($a_T$)?
What is the direction of the angular velocity vector?
a: Perpendicular to the plane of rotation and along the axis of rotation.
Tangent to the plane of rotation.
Radial, pointing away from the center.

q: How is the direction of angular velocity usually specified?
a: By the direction of linear velocity.
By the direction of centripetal acceleration.

q: When using the right-hand rule for angular velocity, what do the fingers represent?
a: The direction of the angular velocity vector.

q: When using the right-hand rule for angular velocity, what does the thumb represent?
a: The direction of the angular velocity vector.

q: When the angular velocity is increasing, what is the direction of the angular acceleration vector?
a: In the same direction as the angular velocity.
In the opposite direction to the angular velocity.
Always towards the center of rotation.

q: When the angular velocity is decreasing, what is the direction of the angular acceleration vector?
a: In the opposite direction to the angular velocity.
In the same direction as the angular velocity.
Always towards the center of rotation.

q: What does "linear velocity" refer to in the context of Key Words?
a: The rate of change of linear displacement.
The rate of change of angular displacement.
The acceleration along a straight line.
The change in direction of motion.

q: What does "tangential acceleration" refer to in the context of Key Words?
a: The acceleration component tangent to the circular path.
The acceleration component radial to the circular path.
The rate of change of angular velocity.

q: What type of motion is described as an object moving in a circle at constant speed $v$?
In uniform circular motion, what remains constant?
a: Both magnitude and direction of velocity.

q: In uniform circular motion, what continuously changes, even if speed is constant?
a: The radius of the circular path.

q: What does the change in direction of velocity constitute?
An object revolving in a circle is continuously accelerating under what condition?
a: Even when the speed remains constant.
Only when its speed is increasing.
Only when its speed is decreasing.
Only when its direction is constant.

q: How is average acceleration defined?
What happens to instantaneous acceleration when $\Delta t$ approaches zero?
When $\Delta t$ is very small in circular motion, what is the relationship between $\Delta \mathbf{v}$ and $\mathbf{v}_1$ (or $\mathbf{v}_2$)?
a: $\Delta \mathbf{v}$ is essentially perpendicular to both of them.
$\Delta \mathbf{v}$ is in the same direction as them.

q: In which direction does the acceleration $\mathbf{a}$ point in circular motion when $\Delta t$ is very small?
a: Toward the center of the circle.
Away from the center of the circle.

q: What are the other names for centripetal acceleration?
What is the symbol for centripetal acceleration?
The magnitude of centripetal acceleration $a_c$ can be obtained using the linear speed $v$ and radius $r$ as:
What is $\lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{\Delta s}{\Delta t}$ in the context of circular motion?
a: The linear speed $v$ of the object.
The angular speed $\omega$ of the object.
The linear acceleration $a$ of the object.
The angular acceleration $\alpha$ of the object.

q: By substituting $v = r \omega$ into the centripetal acceleration formula, what is the alternative expression for $a_c$?
For uniform circular motion, in what direction does the centripetal acceleration vector point?
a: Towards the center of the circular path.
Away from the center of the circular path.

q: For uniform circular motion, what is the direction of the linear velocity vector?
a: Towards the center of the circular path.
Away from the center of the circular path.

q: How are centripetal acceleration and linear velocity related in terms of direction?
a: They are perpendicular to each other at every point in the path.
They are parallel to each other.
They are independent of each other.

q: What characterizes an object moving in nonuniform circular motion?
a: It moves in a circular path with a varying speed (magnitude of velocity).
It moves in a straight line with constant speed.
It moves in a circular path with constant speed.
It moves in an elliptical path.

q: In nonuniform circular motion, what happens to the angular speed of the object?
What does an object experience if its angular speed is changing in nonuniform circular motion?
What does angular acceleration give rise to in nonuniform circular motion?
In nonuniform circular motion, what types of acceleration are present?
a: Tangential acceleration in addition to centripetal acceleration.

q: What is the resultant acceleration of an object in nonuniform circular motion?
a: The vector sum of the centripetal and tangential accelerations.
The scalar sum of the centripetal and tangential accelerations.
The difference between the centripetal and tangential accelerations.
The product of the centripetal and tangential accelerations.

q: What is the vector equation for resultant acceleration $\mathbf{a}$ in nonuniform circular motion?
How are the two acceleration vectors $\mathbf{a}_c$ and $\mathbf{a}_T$ oriented with respect to each other?
a: They are perpendicular to each other.
They are parallel to each other.
They are in the same direction.

q: In which direction is the centripetal acceleration vector $\mathbf{a}_c$?
a: Perpendicular to the plane of rotation.

q: In which direction is the tangential acceleration vector $\mathbf{a}_T$?
a: Perpendicular to the plane of rotation.

q: What is the magnitude of resultant acceleration $a$?
How is the direction of resultant acceleration $\mathbf{a}$ expressed in terms of $\phi$?
Which type of acceleration is present in *both* uniform and nonuniform circular motion?
Which type of acceleration is *unlike* tangential acceleration in its presence in both uniform and nonuniform circular motion?
What does "uniform circular motion" mean according to the Key Words?
a: Motion in a circle at constant speed.
Motion in a circle with varying speed.

q: What does "nonuniform circular motion" mean according to the Key Words?
a: Motion in a circle with varying speed.
Motion in a circle at constant speed.
Rotation at a constant angular velocity.

q: What does "centripetal acceleration" refer to in the Key Words?
a: Acceleration directed towards the center of a circular path.
Acceleration tangent to a circular path.
Acceleration along the axis of rotation.

q: What does "tangential acceleration" refer to in the Key Words?
a: Acceleration component tangent to the circular path.
Acceleration component radial to the circular path.
Acceleration that changes the direction of velocity.

q: What condition implies that angular acceleration is constant, leading to rotational motion under constant angular acceleration?
a: If the angular velocity of the rotating object changes at a constant rate.
If the linear velocity of the rotating object changes at a constant rate.
If the object is at rest.
If the object moves in a straight line.

q: In uniform circular motion, where does the centripetal acceleration vector point?
a: Towards the centre of the circular path.
Away from the centre of the circular path.

q: In uniform circular motion, what is the direction of the linear velocity vector?
a: Towards the centre of the circular path.
Away from the centre of the circular path.
Perpendicular to the plane of motion.

q: What is the name of the acceleration described as pointing towards the center of the circular path with a linear velocity vector tangential to the path in uniform circular motion?
Which of the following describes rotational motion?
a: An object rotates about an axis.
An object just moves in a circle.
An object moves without changing its orientation.
An object translates along a straight line.
